_description: Tier 1 scenario family weights. Maps a MarketState (carry regime, target distance, tenor, vol) to a weight per scenario family. Weights are then applied to per-family mean P&L to produce a single weighted P&L score per structure. Same architecture as affinity_scores.json: baseline + scaled adjustments, normalised, with a floor.
_what_the_weights_mean: Each weight expresses how much we believe a given scenario family represents what the market is likely to do — given the trade view and the current market state. They are NOT structure-dependent: the same vector is applied to every shortlisted structure so that resulting scores are directly comparable.
_baseline_logic: All 8 families start at 1/8 = 0.125 (equal-weighted, no view). Rules nudge individual families up or down based on conditions evaluated against MarketState. After all rules fire, weights are floored (no family below `floor`) and renormalised to sum to 1.

_rule_schema.id: Stable identifier — used to surface which rules fired in the UI / logs.
_rule_schema.comment: Plain-English description of why this rule exists. Read this when tuning.
_rule_schema.when: Array of {field, op, value} — ALL must be true for the rule to fire. Supported fields: target_z_abs, carry_regime, with_carry, T, vol, atmfsratio. Supported ops: '>', '>=', '<', '<=', '==', '!='. If a referenced field is None on MarketState (e.g. target_z when no target supplied), the condition evaluates false.
_rule_schema.family: One of the 8 scenario families above.
_rule_schema.adjustment: Signed delta added to that family's baseline weight. Positive = bump up, negative = bump down.

rules.comment: Target is far from forward (>1.5σ). To get there, the market must move with conviction — and likely overshoot. Bump OVERSHOOT to reflect that fat-tail behaviour matters more here than an early stall at the target.
Mirror of the rule above: if the target is far away, the chance of hitting it early is lower than the baseline assumes. Trim EARLY_TARGET.

Target is close to forward (<0.5σ) — easily reachable on normal random walk. The market is likely to oscillate around it; early touches and pull-backs become the dominant scenarios. Bump EARLY_TARGET.
Mirror: when the target is close, big overshoots are less informative — they're just noise around an easy level. Trim OVERSHOOT.
High carry (regime 2) trading with the carry: drift is your friend. CORRECT_PATH (smooth move toward target) is the most likely realised outcome.
High carry with the trade — wrong-way moves are heavily penalised by the drift. WRONG_WAY scenarios are less likely; trim their weight.

High carry against the trade: the carry is a constant headwind. The realised path is far more likely to drift the wrong way. Bump WRONG_WAY heavily — this is the family the PM should be paying attention to.
High carry against the trade also raises the relative weight of NO_MOVE: a flat market with negative drift will close in adverse territory. Bump NO_MOVE.
Carry regime 0 = noisy. With no clear drift, NO_MOVE is no longer the privileged scenario it would be under medium/high carry. Trim it slightly.
When carry signal is weak, vol-driven mean reversion dominates. Lift VOL_SENSITIVITY so PnL outcomes that hinge on the vol surface get appropriate weight.

Tenor < 1m: theta and pin-risk dominate. EXPIRY (intrinsic value at maturity) is the family the PM should weight heaviest because there is no time for path scenarios to differentiate from terminal outcome.
Conversely, with very short tenor there's barely time for a meaningful 'early target' touch to materially differ from terminal P&L. Trim EARLY_TARGET.
Tenor > 6m: lots of room for the spot to wander to the target and back. EARLY_TARGET (touching, then drifting away) becomes a real risk for capped structures. Bump it.
ATM vol > 20%: vol moves materially affect option value beyond directional P&L. Lift VOL_SENSITIVITY so vega-rich structures (long gamma, long vol) get appropriately credited.
